instruction:
One of the most feared predators in the ocean is the great white shark. It is known that the white shark grows to a mean length of $19$ feet; however, one marine biologist believes that great white sharks off the Bermuda coast grow much longer. To test this claim, full-grown white sharks were captured, measured, and then set free. However, this was a difficult, costly and very dangerous task, so only four sharks were actually sampled. Their lengths were $25, \ 24, \ 24, \mbox{and} \ 27$ feet. Do the data provide sufficient evidence to support the claim? Use $\alpha=0.05$.
(a) Calculate the test statistic: t=[ANS]
(b) Find the critical value: t*=[ANS]
(c) The final conclusion is [ANS] A. We can reject the null hypothesis that the average length of the shark is $19$.  B. There is not sufficient evidence to reject the null hypothesis that the average length of the shark is $19$.
